critic_raw:
This is a well-designed experiment overall. The temporal split is appropriate, the labeling pipeline is multi-source and conservative, the ablations are logically structured, and the metric choice (AUPRC over ROC-AUC) is justified. I'll work through genuine concerns rather than manufactured ones.

**Issue 1: Sender reputation leakage via the graph construction window**

The methodology states that sender reputation scores and graph context are 'computed from historical sending patterns' and 'derived from communications prior to the email send time.' This is stated as a control but not operationalized with sufficient precision. The critical question is: what is the reference window for 'historical'?

If reputation scores and graph edge weights are computed globally from the entire training corpus (months 1–12) and then applied during feature generation — including for emails within the training set itself — earlier emails in the training window are being scored using pattern information from later emails that had not yet occurred at the time those emails arrived. This is within-training-set temporal leakage. It does not affect train/test separation, but it does mean the model trains on a distribution of features that is richer than what will exist at inference time, particularly for novel senders and low-volume correspondents who accumulate reputation signal slowly. In deployment, an email arriving in month 1 of operation would have almost no sender reputation history, whereas the trained model may have learned to heavily weight reputation features that were computed with months of hindsight. The result would be optimistic validation and test performance because both validation and test sets also benefit from having training-set history to draw on — specifically, the 12-month reputation window preceding each validation or test email is well-populated — but this degrades gracefully at cold-start inference time only if the model learned to be robust to sparse reputation, which it has no incentive to do.

Evidence one way or another: Report model performance stratified by sender age (first email seen in training data vs. senders with >30 days / >90 days of history). If performance drops sharply for senders with thin history, this is the mechanism. A clean fix is to simulate reputation state as of each email's send time using only emails strictly prior to it, implemented as a rolling computation.

**Issue 2: Graph context structural asymmetry between proposed model and enriched baseline**

The control design in Section 7 includes 'baseline enrichment' where the baseline receives engineered graph statistics as numeric features. However, the proposed model receives 'local neighborhood structure (1–2 hops)' via graph attention layers. The distinction is that graph attention over a neighborhood is a permutation-equivariant operation that can represent relational patterns (e.g., 'this sender has communicated with 3 recipients who have all received malicious emails this week') that aggregate statistics flatten into scalar summaries (e.g., 'sender degree = 47'). If the proposed model's advantage derives from this relational structure rather than from the transformer architecture on text, the ablation as described cannot separate these two things. A 'text-only transformer vs. full transformer' ablation will conflate text modeling capacity with graph attention capacity. The enriched baseline comparison then becomes the key test, but it may not be a fair one if the graph statistics engineered for the baseline are coarser than the information the graph attention layer implicitly captures.

Evidence: The ablation design should include a model that receives graph attention but no transformer text encoder (e.g., a GNN over sender-recipient relationships with simple text features), and separately a transformer with text only and no graph component. The current two-point ablation (text-only transformer vs. full model) leaves the graph contribution unattributed. Without this, the methodology cannot support claims about whether the gain is architectural (transformer) or relational (graph).

**Issue 3: Label construction introduces survivorship bias correlated with model features**

Labeling source (4) is 'post-incident response records linking compromised user accounts to specific emails.' This means some positive labels are only assigned retroactively when a compromise was detected. The detection of compromises is not independent of the features used by the model. Specifically, compromises are more likely to be detected and traced back to a specific email when: (a) the email was sent from a known malicious sender (high sender reputation signal), (b) the email was part of a multi-recipient campaign (detectable via graph structure), or (c) the email contained identifiable payload characteristics.

This means the positive class is disproportionately labeled on examples where the model's features are most diagnostic, making the task artificially easier than the real-world problem (catching novel, stealthy attacks that don't trigger incident response). The baseline text-only model is less affected by this because it doesn't use the features most correlated with detection, so the transformer's apparent advantage may partly reflect that it's evaluated on a positive class that was selected using features it has access to.

Evidence: Report separately the performance on positive examples derived from each labeling source. If incident-response-derived positives (source 4) show substantially higher model scores than analyst-review-derived positives (source 1) that were not associated with incidents, the survivorship bias is operative.

**Summary:**

Issues 1 and 3 are genuine methodological flaws with specific failure mechanisms. Issue 2 is a design gap that limits the interpretability of the ablation results but does not invalidate the comparison of final model performance. Issues 1 and 3 both work in the same direction: inflating measured performance on the malicious class in ways that are correlated with the proposed model's unique features, meaning they inflate the apparent advantage of the proposed model over the baseline more than they inflate the baseline's performance. None of these are fatal to the experiment if addressed; issue 1 has a clean fix, issue 3 requires a stratified analysis to bound the effect, and issue 2 requires an additional ablation condition.
all_issues_raised: Sender reputation and graph features may be computed with within-window temporal leakage (using future-of-email-timestamp history within the training period), creating a feature distribution at training time that is richer than at cold-start inference and inflating measured performance on senders with thin history.

The two-point ablation (text-only transformer vs. full model) cannot separate the contribution of graph attention from transformer text encoding; the enriched baseline comparison is not a fair substitute because engineered scalar graph statistics are informationally coarser than graph attention over neighborhoods.

Positive labels derived from post-incident response (source 4) are selectively assigned to emails detectable via features the proposed model uses (sender reputation, graph structure), creating survivorship bias that makes the positive class artificially easier for the transformer than for the text-only baseline.
all_issues_adjudicated: Sender reputation and graph features may be computed with within-window temporal leakage (using future-of-email-timestamp history within the training period), creating a feature distribution at training time that is richer than at cold-start inference and inflating measured performance on senders with thin history.

The two-point ablation (text-only transformer vs. full model) cannot separate the contribution of graph attention from transformer text encoding; the enriched baseline comparison is not a fair substitute because engineered scalar graph statistics are informationally coarser than graph attention over neighborhoods.

Positive labels derived from post-incident response (source 4) are selectively assigned to emails detectable via features the proposed model uses (sender reputation, graph structure), creating survivorship bias that makes the positive class artificially easier for the transformer than for the text-only baseline.
